text: > Keep an eye out for Zach Edey, he’s starting to settle into the NBA game and opposing frontcourts are already having issues with him in the paint. That's kind of the problem. Modern defense isn't "allowed to play defense". So you have to send multiple people to stop a layup. Which means there's always a theoretical window where a 3pt shooter is open. And you wonder why 3pt attempts are at an all time high.   Not because players have unguardable stepback 3's (well a little bit of that). But because even a player like Gobert, is a threat in the paint, and requires 2 players collapsing on him.

text: Amateur Scouting Accuracy, League History. Using win-shares, we find that it is very rare for the NBA to successfully identify the top draft prospects. There is only one season in which all three of the best players were drafted within the top three picks. In 31% of drafts none of the top three picks wind up among the three-best players from that draft.

text: He was a special perimeter defender with his length and speed. The problem was that he wasn’t great at the rim which severely limited his value. He’d be a watered down Draymond if he could protect the rim to a high level, even with his lack of a jumper or Drays IQ. He pushed the pace in transition well and while not as good a passer as Draymond, he was decent. I do also think Simmons was drafted to a team that was all wrong for him considering his refusal to improve on offense. To build around a traditional big man like Embiid, Shaq, Hakeem, even Duncan, you need shooting and it needs to be excellent. History shows that. Robert Horry, Steve Smith, Bruce Bowen, Jaren Jackson, Mario Elie, Derek Anderson, Derrick Fisher, Rick Fox etc. The sixers have lacked these quality 3&D wings. 94 and 95 Rockets were by far the best 3pt shooters in the league.
label: analysis
